title: Paradigm Conflict in Water Physics: When Measurement Methods Embed Theoretical Assumptions
author: McGinn, James
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: This paper examines a fundamental epistemological challenge to mainstream water physics posed by James McGinn's "proximity switch" theory, which proposes that hydrogen bonds progressively cancel molecular polarity in discrete steps. While McGinn's model provides unified mechanistic explanations for water's three major "anomalies" (low viscosity, high surface tension, and exceptional heat capacity) that orthodox theory treats as separate phenomena requiring ad-hoc patches, the deeper significance lies in McGinn's critique of scientific methodology itself. He argues that the quantitative measurement apparatus supporting orthodox water science was designed using the theoretical assumptions it claims to test, creating a self-reinforcing paradigm where alternative theories cannot receive fair evaluation. This case study illuminates broader problems in how science evaluates competing paradigms when measurement methods are theory-laden, raising critical questions about the reliability of established quantitative frameworks and the institutional barriers that may prevent recognition of explanatorily superior theories. The analysis concludes with actionable recommendations for methodological reforms to address paradigm lock-in and ensure that scientific institutions can fairly evaluate theories that challenge fundamental assumptions about measurement and interpretation.
